title: The Achilles’ heel of Salience theory and a way to fix it
abstract: Salience theory has been successfully used to explain a wide range of empirical and experimental phenomena such as the Allais paradox, framing effect, the preference reversal phenomenon or the decoy and compromise effects. In this paper we show that salience theory carries a notable flaw and under certain circumstances it suggests that a salient thinker may prefer a dominated option even when a strictly dominant alternative is available to her. To solve this problem we propose a possible alteration of the theory and show how it accounts for the same phenomena as the salience theory.
summary_en: Salience theory explains many behavioural puzzles (Allais paradox, framing effects, preference reversals). But does it suffer from a theoretical flaw? This paper shows that under certain circumstances, salience theory predicts that a decision-maker could prefer a dominated option over a dominant one — a violation of basic rationality. The authors propose a fix and show the modified theory still explains the same behavioural phenomena. A useful technical repair to a widely-used behavioural model.
policy_relevance: Primarily theoretical. Indirectly relevant to behavioural-economics applications in Hungarian consumer-protection analysis. Caveat: methodological; no direct policy application.
